title: Alcea flavovirens
intro: Alcea flavovirens, also known as the golden hollyhock, is a perennial plant species belonging to the mallow family (Malvaceae). The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may aid in wound healing, reducing inflammation, and protecting against oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Alcea flavovirens include flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, as well as alkaloids and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Alcea flavovirens is used in traditional medicine, and its bioactive compounds are often extracted and formulated into herbal preparations, such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, to harness its therapeutic potential.
taxonomy: Alcea flavovirens belongs to the family Malvaceae and is classified as a species of flowering plant. It is native to the mountainous regions of western China. This plant is a member of the genus Alcea, which includes several species of hollyhocks. Alcea flavovirens is further classified into the subgenus Alcea, which is characterized by its large, showy flowers. The species is also known to produce seeds that are rich in nutrients and are an important food source for local wildlife.
uses: Alcea flavovirens is known for its potential medicinal properties, although more research is needed to confirm its uses. Alcea flavovirens contains flavonoids, alkaloids, and other bioactive compounds, which contribute to its therapeutic effects.
